title: Sudden Purple Bruising on a Cat's Belly: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Purple bruises on your cat's belly without an injury are a high-priority emergency. Learn about clotting risks and why you must see a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Unexplained bruising on a cat is a life-threatening medical emergency that indicates a serious systemic clotting issue. Seek immediate veterinary care and take a photo of the area to help the medical team track the progression of the condition.

What does it mean if my cat has unexplained bruising on its abdomen?

Finding large, purple, or reddish patches on your cat’s abdomen when no trauma has occurred is a deeply concerning sign. In veterinary medicine, these marks are often referred to as ecchymosis (large bruises) or petechiae (pinpoint spots). These are not typical bruises from a bump; they indicate that blood is leaking from the vessels into the skin, which points to a systemic failure in your cat’s blood clotting mechanism.

Is unexplained bruising in cats considered a high-priority emergency?

  • Treat this situation as a high-priority medical emergency.
  • Recognize that bruising without injury suggests internal issues that could lead to life-threatening bleeding.
  • Transport your cat to the nearest emergency veterinary clinic immediately.
  • Do not wait for a scheduled appointment, as clotting disorders can worsen in a matter of hours.

Why is taking a photo of my cat's bruises critical for triage?

  • Take a clear, well-lit photo of the bruised area before or during transit to the clinic.
  • Document the area quickly, as bruising can shift, expand, or change color rapidly.
  • Provide a timestamped visual record to show the veterinary team how fast the condition is progressing.
  • Use this documentation as a vital triage tool to help the medical team prioritize your cat's care upon arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Cats presenting with sudden, unexplained purple bruising on the abdomen should be triaged for possible intra-abdominal or body wall hemorrhage. Critically assess for abdominal pain and signs of shock, which are considered indicative of intra-abdominal hemorrhage until proven otherwise. Common sources of abdominal hemorrhage include splenic or hepatic rupture/laceration, avulsed mesenteric vessels, damaged muscle, or kidney avulsion in the retroperitoneal space; hemorrhage into the GI system or retroperitoneal/fascial hemorrhage can be more challenging to identify. Diagnostic evaluation should include abdominal palpation, abdominocentesis (fluid analysis for PCV, WBCs, plant/meat fibers, bacteria, creatinine, potassium, bilirubin, amylase, phosphorus, and glucose compared to peripheral blood), FAST scan, and radiographs to assess for free fluid and organ damage. Ongoing hemorrhage, inability to stabilize shock, or evidence of peritonitis necessitates emergency exploratory laparotomy.

Chapter: Emergency, Surgery, Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1670)

faqs.question: What could cause my cat to bruise without an injury?
faqs.answer: Common causes include the ingestion of rodenticides (rat poison), immune-mediated diseases where the body attacks its own platelets, severe infections, or liver failure.

faqs.question: Are there other symptoms I should look for?
faqs.answer: Yes. Check for pale or white gums, lethargy, blood in the urine or stool, or tiny red dots on the insides of the ears and gums.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat any over-the-counter medicine?
faqs.answer: No. Never give a bruising cat any medication without veterinary approval. Common human medications like aspirin can severely worsen bleeding and lead to fatal complications.
